<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: unified-adaptive-tribunal-and-adversarial-review
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/unified-adaptive-tribunal-and-adversarial-review.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/unified-adaptive-tribunal-and-adversarial-review.qmd@89c8d600d
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Unified Adaptive Tribunal and Adversarial Review

Claim ledgers remember what the system believes. Spinoza asks what kind of
justification a claim needs before it can become stronger. The tribunal exists
for the claims and artifacts that are still too contested, too high-stakes, or
too ambiguous for one verifier result.

The tribunal is not a machine for manufacturing certainty. It is an escalation
protocol. A claim enters with a dossier, a risk class, a boundary around what
evidence is in scope, and a reason ordinary verification was not enough. It
leaves as an accepted claim within limits, a revision order, a blocked claim,
an expanded retrieval request, a human adjudication request, or a residual that
later work must carry.

The reader throughline is simple: review must change state.

That is the difference between serious adversarial review and decorative
critique. A review that produces a paragraph of concerns but changes no claim,
plan, permission, residual, or required action is not part of the stack. It is
commentary. A tribunal verdict should become a ledger update or an execution
constraint, or it should admit that it did not settle the matter.

## Problem

Some decisions are too consequential for a single model's confidence.

The stack can already hold claim identity in a ledger and ask Spinoza for a
proof-carrying, citation-carrying, procedure-carrying, or review-carrying
justification. But there are claims where the hard part is not only whether an
artifact exists. The hard part is whether the evidence boundary is adequate,
whether the interpretation is contested, whether the stakes require dissent to
remain visible, and whether unresolved objections should block downstream
work.

Those cases need more than a pass/fail verifier. They need a bounded review
process that can assign roles, stress the claim from different angles, preserve
minority objections, and return an action that the rest of the system must
respect.

The tribunal is that process. It does not replace proof, source review,
benchmarks, or human judgment. It coordinates them when a claim is important
enough that the system needs an auditable disagreement record before moving.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Self-critique is cheap.

A model can criticize its own answer and still share the same blind spots that
produced the answer. Multiple model reviewers can also collapse into consensus
theater if they receive the same evidence, optimize for agreement, or fail to
record what each role actually tested. A committee is not automatically more
truthful than one reviewer. Sometimes it is only a larger surface for confident
language.

The failure mode is review laundering. A claim gets rejected, revised, or
blocked, then it is sent through another review pass over the same evidence
until the prose sounds measured enough to pass. Nothing changed except tone.
The tribunal record exists to make that path visible. It records prior review
references, the unchanged-evidence guard, dissent, unresolved issues, required
actions, and constraint effects.

The external contestable-AI literature is useful here because it keeps the
chapter grounded in a known governance problem: affected parties need ways to
challenge, appeal, audit, and dispute AI-mediated decisions. In this book, that
maps to tribunal dossiers, dissent preservation, appeal routes, and required
actions. It does not prove that this repository has implemented a contestable
institution or validated a tribunal process.

## Core Claim

A tribunal layer should coordinate multiple reviewers, adversarial probes,
consensus rules, and documented uncertainty. This remains an architectural
argument.

The source basis is bounded. UAT supplies the retrieval-bounded proposition
review pattern: structural priors, retrieval dossiers, atomic proposition
states, adversarial siege, hard cycle caps, and human checkpoints. Spinoza
supplies the tier and downgrade consequences when verification fails, times
out, or exposes ambiguity. Talos supplies the execution pressure: review
outputs have to become typed, auditable artifacts with claim graphs, evidence
sets, proof bundles, logs, replay, residual risk, and human adjudication.
Verification Bandwidth explains why tribunal escalation is sometimes the right
answer to inadequate verification workspace rather than simply adding more
context. Coherence Exchange remains speculative connector/source-note framing,
not implemented institutional evidence.

The live chapter core claim stays at `argument` support. The repository has a
tribunal-review schema, synthetic fixtures, a local harness, and finite Lean
predicates over record fields. It does not have a working multi-reviewer UAT
pipeline, measured reviewer independence, validated adversarial probe quality,
validated verdict correctness, institutional contestability, runtime behavior,
or support-state movement for the chapter core claim.

## Mechanism

The tribunal begins when ordinary verification is not enough.

An escalated target enters with a `target_ref`, `risk_class`,
`dossier_boundary`, and `dossier_refs`. The dossier boundary matters because a
tribunal is not allowed to quietly use hidden evidence and then present its
verdict as dossier-bounded. If retrieval expands, the expansion has to be
recorded as part of the review.

The next move is role separation. Review roles can include logic critic,
citation critic, omission critic, safety critic, implementation critic, domain
reviewer, or human adjudicator. The point is not to multiply voices. The point
is to create different attack surfaces and record which one found what.

Then come adversarial probes. A probe can test whether the claim follows from
the dossier, whether sources actually support the interpretation, whether an
important omitted case changes the result, whether a safety constraint is being
weakened, or whether the proposed action should be blocked until a human
decides.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Escalated claim or artifact"] --> B["Bounded review dossier"]
  B --> C["Role-separated reviewers"]
  C --> D["Adversarial probes"]
  D --> E["Evidence-linked findings"]
  E --> F{"Verdict changes state?"}
  F -- "accept within scope" --> G["Ledger update with limits"]
  F -- "revise / reject / block" --> H["Required action"]
  F -- "not settled" --> I["Dissent, residual, or retrieval expansion"]
  G --> J["Planning and execution constraints"]
  H --> J
  I --> J
```

The diagram's important question is not whether the tribunal reached
agreement. It is whether the verdict changed the state of the stack. An
accepted claim should carry scope limits. A revision should create required
work. A rejection should block promotion. A blocked result should stop dispatch
or narrow authority. An unresolved result should preserve dissent, residuals,
or retrieval expansion needs.

The tribunal record is therefore an interface object, not a transcript. It
records reviewer roles, adversarial probes, findings, evidence references,
dissent, unresolved issues, verdict, required actions, constraint effects,
human adjudication, and non-claims. The review can be discussed in prose, but
the state change has to be machine-readable.

## Interfaces

Spinoza sends claims to the tribunal when proof, citation, procedure, or replay
artifacts are not enough to settle the risk. A semantic mismatch, unresolved
ambiguity, missing evidence, contested interpretation, or high-impact decision
can all become tribunal routes.

Planning sends work to the tribunal when a plan node would otherwise cross a
risk boundary without enough review. A tribunal result can block dispatch,
narrow adapter permissions, require a source fetch, create a residual task, or
ask for human sign-off.

Claim ledgers consume the result. They store whether the claim was accepted
within a scope, downgraded, blocked, revised, or left unresolved. They also
store the dissent and unresolved issues so future work cannot erase them by
restating the claim more smoothly.

A practical tribunal-review record needs at least these fields:

- `review_id`
- `target_ref`
- `review_state`
- `risk_class`
- `dossier_boundary`
- `dossier_refs`
- `reviewer_independence`
- `reviewer_roles`
- `adversarial_probes`
- `cycle_cap`
- `prior_review_refs`
- `unchanged_evidence_guard`
- `retrieval_expansion_policy`
- `findings`
- `evidence_refs`
- `dissent`
- `unresolved_issues`
- `verdict`
- `required_actions`
- `constraint_effects`
- `human_adjudication`
- `non_claims`

These fields do not prove that reviewers are independent or that a verdict is
correct. They make it harder to hide when those properties are missing.

## Invariants

Dissent is preserved.

Consensus requires evidence.

High-risk review needs enough role separation, probes, and dossier references
to be audited later.

Those are modest invariants, but they are useful. They prevent a verdict from
being treated as finished when it has no evidence references, no recorded
roles, no adversarial probes, or no unresolved-issue record. They also prevent
the most dangerous version of review laundering: repeating review over the same
evidence and treating a cleaner consensus statement as new support.

Scope is part of the invariant. A tribunal can accept a claim only inside the
scope it reviewed. If the claim later appears in a broader context, the ledger
has to carry the original limits forward or require a new review.

## Failure Modes

The first failure mode is collusion. Reviewers can collude explicitly, but they
can also collude structurally by sharing prompts, hidden assumptions, context
gaps, incentives, or blind spots. A tribunal record should make role collapse
visible before its verdict binds downstream work.

The second failure mode is consensus theater. Agreement without evidence can
look like progress because it feels socially settled. The record has to ask
what findings, evidence refs, and probes actually support the verdict.

The third failure mode is critique without action. A review that notices a
problem and then allows the same claim, plan, permission, or release to move
unchanged is ornamental. Required actions and constraint effects are the
discipline that turns critique into governance.

The fourth failure mode is tribunal overuse. If every uncertain sentence
requires a tribunal, the stack becomes too slow to operate. Tribunal review
belongs at escalation points: high-risk artifacts, contested claims,
proof-mismatch cases, unresolved value conflicts, safety-relevant plan nodes,
and places where ordinary verification lacks adequate workspace.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable tribunal is a schema and fixture set for review records.

The current repository already has that narrow slice: a
`tribunal_review_record` schema, valid and expected-invalid fixtures, and a
validator. The harness checks record discipline around high-risk reviewer
roles, adversarial probes, dossier refs, accept verdict evidence refs, dissent
preservation, prior-review laundering, required actions, constraint effects,
and explicit non-claims.

That is useful, but it is not a tribunal pipeline. It validates the envelope,
not the quality of the reviewers inside it. It does not measure independence,
probe quality, consensus quality, verdict correctness, human adjudication, or
runtime behavior.

The next honest implementation step would be a small contested-claim rehearsal:
one high-risk claim that cannot be accepted without review, one tribunal record
with role-separated probes and preserved dissent, one verdict that expands
retrieval rather than promoting the claim, and one repeated review attempt
blocked because the evidence set did not change.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature tribunal is an operating system for contested claims and high-risk
artifacts.

In that system, ordinary verification handles routine support. Tribunal review
handles escalation. Dossier construction tools define the evidence boundary.
Role templates assign logic, citation, omission, implementation, security,
governance, and domain reviewers. Probe workflows run with cycle caps. Dissent
and unresolved issues remain attached to the verdict. Required actions become
ledger updates, source requests, authority narrowing, plan blocks, residual
tasks, or human adjudication.

The mature endpoint is not more debate. It is more consequential review. The
tribunal can say: accept within this scope, revise this claim, reject this
artifact, block this dispatch, narrow this permission, fetch this source, ask
this human, or carry this residual. Re-running review over unchanged evidence
cannot quietly promote the claim because the prior-review guard exposes the
attempt.

That endpoint is still target architecture. The book can describe it, and the
repository can validate small record-level pieces, but no chapter prose should
pretend the mature tribunal exists here today.

## Proof and Test Boundary

The Lean module for this chapter proves finite-record gates. It can show that
a valid issued verdict record includes reviewer roles, evidence references, and
unresolved-dissent recording. It can also show that a high-risk artifact
missing required tribunal review cannot be accepted under the modeled record.

Those are record properties. They do not prove reviewer independence,
adversarial-probe quality, verdict correctness, consensus quality, human
adjudication quality, runtime behavior, institutional contestability, or
source interpretation.

The tribunal harness has a similarly narrow boundary. It passed locally over
three valid fixtures and five expected-invalid fixtures. That result supports
synthetic record discipline only. It does not promote the chapter core claim.

## Summary

Unified Adaptive Tribunal and Adversarial Review gives the stack a way to
escalate claims that ordinary verification cannot responsibly settle. It uses
dossier boundaries, role-separated reviewers, adversarial probes,
evidence-linked findings, dissent, unresolved issues, verdicts, required
actions, and human adjudication hooks.

The important rule is that review must change state. It should update a ledger,
block a claim, revise an artifact, narrow authority, demand a source, preserve
dissent, or create a residual. If nothing changes except the presence of a
critique paragraph, the tribunal layer has failed.

## Handoff

Tribunal review only matters if its decisions alter downstream work. Labor OS
and Typed Jobs turns accepted, narrowed, blocked, or revised decisions into
controlled units of execution with lifecycle state, permissions, artifacts,
audit events, replay declarations, and failure behavior. That is where
deliberation becomes governed labor instead of another recommendation.
